questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an isotope of Hydrogen?
questions.explanation: Helium (He) is a different element. Protium (¹H), Deuterium (²H), and Tritium (³H) are isotopes of Hydrogen.

questions.text: The most abundant isotope of Hydrogen is:
questions.explanation: Protium (¹H) constitutes over 99.98% of natural hydrogen.

questions.text: Tritium is a radioactive isotope of Hydrogen with a half-life of approximately:
questions.explanation: Tritium (³H) decays by beta emission with a half-life of 12.3 years.

questions.text: Which of the following methods is used for the industrial preparation of Hydrogen?
options: Reaction of active metals with acids
Reaction of steam with red hot coke
questions.explanation: The reaction of steam with red hot coke (C + H₂O → CO + H₂) is a common industrial method (water-gas shift reaction).

questions.text: Hydrogen resembles alkali metals in forming:
questions.explanation: Like alkali metals, hydrogen can lose one electron to form H⁺ ion.

questions.text: Which type of hydride is formed by s-block elements?
questions.explanation: s-block elements (Group 1 and 2) are highly electropositive and form ionic (saline) hydrides.

questions.text: Water gas is a mixture of:
questions.explanation: Water gas (or syngas) is a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen.

questions.text: Permanent hardness of water is due to the presence of:
options: Chlorides and sulphates of Ca and Mg
questions.explanation: Permanent hardness is caused by dissolved chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium.

questions.text: Which of the following methods is used to remove permanent hardness of water?
questions.explanation: Calgon method (using sodium hexametaphosphate) is an effective way to remove permanent hardness.

questions.text: Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) is a powerful:
questions.explanation: H₂O₂ can act as both an oxidizing agent (e.g., in acidic medium) and a reducing agent (e.g., with strong oxidizing agents).

questions.text: The bond angle in water (H₂O) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Due to lone pair-bond pair repulsion, the angle is slightly less than tetrahedral.

questions.text: Heavy water (D₂O) is used as a _____ in nuclear reactors.
questions.explanation: It slows down neutrons without absorbing them significantly.

questions.text: Which of the following is a non-stoichiometric hydride?
questions.explanation: Metallic hydrides often have non-stoichiometric compositions.

questions.text: Hydrogen can be prepared in the laboratory by the reaction of:
questions.explanation: Active metals like Zn react with dilute acids to produce hydrogen gas.

questions.text: The structure of H₂O₂ in the gaseous phase is:
questions.explanation: It has an open book-like non-planar structure.

questions.text: Which of the following is a use of Hydrogen?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen has diverse applications in industry and as a potential clean fuel.

questions.text: Temporary hardness of water is due to the presence of:
questions.explanation: It can be removed by boiling.

questions.text: The process of converting hard water into soft water is called:
questions.explanation: Water softening involves removing the ions that cause hardness.

questions.text: Hydrogen acts as a reducing agent in the reaction:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen reduces copper oxide to copper.

questions.text: The term 'hydrolith' refers to:
questions.explanation: Calcium hydride (CaH₂) is commonly known as hydrolith and is used as a portable source of hydrogen.

questions.text: Which of the following is an electron-deficient hydride?
questions.explanation: Boron hydrides (like BH₃) have fewer electrons than required to form normal covalent bonds.

questions.text: The reaction of hydrogen with halogens is most vigorous with:
questions.explanation: Fluorine is the most reactive halogen.

questions.text: Hydrogen can be adsorbed by transition metals. This property is called:
questions.explanation: Occlusion is the process by which certain metals (like Pd, Pt) absorb large volumes of hydrogen.

questions.text: The percentage of hydrogen in the universe is approximately:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe.

questions.text: Which of the following is used as an antiseptic?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide is used as a mild antiseptic and disinfectant.

questions.text: The auto-protolysis of water is represented by:
questions.explanation: Water acts as both an acid and a base, forming hydronium and hydroxide ions.

questions.text: The oxidation state of oxygen in H₂O₂ is:
questions.explanation: In peroxides, oxygen has an oxidation state of -1.

questions.text: Which of the following is a covalent hydride?
questions.explanation: Non-metals form covalent hydrides.

questions.text: The reaction of hydrogen with oxygen to form water is:
questions.explanation: It releases a large amount of energy.

questions.text: The process of hydrogenation is used to convert:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen is added across double bonds in unsaturated fats.

questions.text: The zeolite method is used for:
questions.explanation: Zeolites exchange Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions with Na⁺ ions.

questions.text: Which of the following is an electron-rich hydride?
questions.explanation: Elements of Group 15-17 form electron-rich hydrides with lone pairs.

questions.text: The commercial production of hydrogen is mainly from:
questions.explanation: Steam reforming of natural gas (hydrocarbons) is the dominant industrial method.

questions.text: Hydrogen can be used in fuel cells to produce:
questions.explanation: Fuel cells convert chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy and water.

questions.text: The reaction of hydrogen with non-metals generally forms:
questions.explanation: Non-metals share electrons with hydrogen to form covalent compounds.

questions.text: The property of hydrogen to absorb on the surface of metals is called:
questions.explanation: Adsorption is a surface phenomenon.

questions.text: Which of the following is a reducing property of H₂O₂?
questions.explanation: In this reaction, H₂O₂ loses electrons (is oxidized) and thus acts as a reducing agent.

questions.text: The term 'syngas' refers to:
questions.explanation: Syngas is a common name for water gas (CO + H₂).

questions.text: Hydrogen is unique because it can:
options: Lose an electron to form H⁺
Gain an electron to form H⁻
Share electrons to form covalent bonds
questions.explanation: Hydrogen exhibits properties similar to alkali metals, halogens, and forms covalent bonds.

questions.text: The process of demineralization of water involves removing:
questions.explanation: Demineralized water is free from all soluble mineral salts, achieved using ion-exchange resins.
